name: Pratt Parsing
summary: Vaughan Pratt's 1973 expression-parsing algorithm: assign a binding power to each operator and dispatch through per-token nud/led functions. Composes naturally with recursive descent.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Vaughan Pratt's 1973 algorithm for expression parsing assigns a "binding power" (integer precedence) to each operator. The parser consists of two functions per token: `nud` (null denotation, for prefix position) and `led` (left denotation, for infix/postfix position). The core loop is remarkably simple:

```
fn parse(min_bp):
    left = nud(next_token())
    while bp(peek()) > min_bp:
        left = led(left, next_token())
    return left
```

The key insight: recursive descent is natural for statements (which start with keywords), and Pratt parsing is natural for expressions (which start with operands). The two compose perfectly — use recursive descent at the statement level, Pratt parsing within expressions.

sources.label: Bob Nystrom — Pratt Parsers: Expression Parsing Made Easy

sections.title: Binding power model
sections.content:
Each token carries an integer binding power and (potentially) two parse functions: `nud` for use in prefix position, `led` for use in infix or postfix position. Higher binding powers bind tighter, so the parser keeps consuming `led` operators while `bp(peek()) > min_bp`. Associativity is encoded by whether the recursive call uses `bp` (right-associative) or `bp + 1` (left-associative).

New operators integrate by adding entries to the token table — no grammar refactoring, no precedence-table cascade. This locality is what makes Pratt parsing extensible.

sections.title: Strengths and where it dominates
sections.content:
The main advantage is extensibility: adding a new operator requires only specifying its binding power and its `led` function. No grammar rewriting or precedence-table refactoring is required. This is why Pratt parsers dominate in language implementations that need to evolve their operator set — including Pratt's own original target (the CGOL language) and modern hand-written compilers across Rust, Swift, V8, and many others.

Bob Nystrom's explanation in "Pratt Parsers: Expression Parsing Made Easy" made the algorithm accessible to a much wider audience than Pratt's original paper, and is the common modern entry point.

sections.title: Trade-offs
sections.content:
Pratt parsing is an expression-parsing strategy, not a full grammar architecture — it handles operator-rich expression forms but does not replace a grammar definition for the surrounding language. It is less declarative than grammar-first approaches: the parser's behavior lives in per-token functions, which can be harder to summarize for documentation or formal analysis.

For languages with a small, fixed operator set (C, C++, Rust expression position) precedence climbing is often less code; for languages that add operators late (Haskell user-defined operators, extensible-syntax systems), Pratt's per-token dispatch is more natural.

sources.label: Bob Nystrom — Pratt Parsers: Expression Parsing Made Easy
